					<description><![CDATA[<p>Micron ups US investments; Apple's $30B deal; Solstice's buy; onsemi divests; diamonds for inspection; fault-tolerant quantum $; analog IMC; patent infringements; Accellera's functional safety standard; 189k workers shortage; USB standards; fab roadblocks.</p>
<p>The post <a href="https://semiengineering.com/chip-industry-week-in-review-146/">Chip Industry Week In Review</a> appeared first on <a href="https://semiengineering.com">Semiconductor Engineering</a>.</p>
]]></description>
										<content:encoded><![CDATA[<h3><strong>Micron</strong></h3>
<p>The memory maker rolled out a slew of announcements this week, including:</p>
<ul>
<li role="heading" aria-level="2" data-uw-rm-heading="level">Raised <a href="https://investors.micron.com/news-releases/news-release-details/micron-accelerates-us-investments-pours-first-concrete-new-york">its planned U.S. investment</a> to more than $250B through 2035, an incremental $50B above what was announced <a href="https://investors.micron.com/news-releases/news-release-details/micron-and-trump-administration-announce-expanded-us-investments">last June</a>, with an ultimate goal of producing 40% of its DRAM in the U.S.;</li>
<li role="heading" aria-level="2" data-uw-rm-heading="level">Planned new investments of <a href="https://investors.micron.com/news-releases/news-release-details/micron-announces-3-billion-strategic-investment-strengthen-us">$3B for U.S. IC supply-chain investments,</a> including $500M in financing for <strong>GlobalWafers</strong>’ 300mm raw silicon wafer facility in Sherman, Texas, along with a 10-year wafer supply agreement;</li>
<li role="heading" aria-level="2" data-uw-rm-heading="level">Entered into <a href="https://investors.micron.com/news-releases/news-release-details/micron-and-ford-sign-strategic-agreement-strengthen-long-term">a long-term strategic customer agreement</a> with <strong>Ford</strong>, providing a supply of memory and storage solutions for the carmaker&#8217;s next-gen vehicle production.</li>
<li>Started construction on its $9.3B expansion of its Hiroshima Prefecture <a href="https://www.japantimes.co.jp/business/2026/07/04/tech/micron-hiroshima-chip-plant/">advanced memory chip</a> facility.</li>
</ul>
<h3><strong>Large Deals</strong></h3>
<ul>
<li><strong>Apple</strong> announced a $30B+ multiyear commitment with <strong>Broadcom</strong> to design and produce <a href="https://www.apple.com/newsroom/2026/07/apple-to-increase-spend-with-broadcom-to-produce-billions-more-us-chips/">custom silicon</a> components and wireless connectivity technologies for a wide range of Apple products. To supply RF and wireless components, Broadcom will undertake a $1.5B expansion of its Fort Collins, Colorado, manufacturing facilities.</li>
<li><strong>Solstice Advanced Materials </strong>agreed to acquire <strong>Element Solutions </strong>for $14.5B, adding Element&#8217;s electronics chemicals portfolio to Solstice&#8217;s specialty-materials business.</li>
<li><strong>SEALSQ</strong> and <strong>GlobalFoundries</strong> will co-develop <a href="https://www.sealsq.com/investors/news-releases/sealsq-and-globalfoundries-partner-to-accelerate-post-quantum-cryptography-and-quantum-computing-technologies">secure semiconductor platforms</a>, focusing on PQC, cryogenic CMOS technology, and trusted chip production.</li>
<li><strong>Onsemi</strong> <a href="https://www.onsemi.com/company/newsroom/news-and-insights/onsemi-advances-fab-right-strategy-with-agreements-to-divest-two-manufacturing-facilities">plans to shed</a> its Tarlac, Philippines manufacturing site to <strong>Greatek Electronics</strong> and its Mountain Top, Pennsylvania site to <strong>Silex Microsystems</strong>.</li>
<li><strong>Sumitomo Chemical’s</strong> subsidiary is teaming up with<strong> Samsung Electro-Mechanics</strong> on <a href="https://www.sumitomo-chem.co.jp/english/news/detail/20260702e_2.html">glass core substrates for advanced packaging</a>.</li>
</ul>
<h3><strong>Raking it in</strong></h3>
<ul>
<li><strong>QuantumDiamonds</strong> received US$104M to ramp up production of its quantum-based semiconductor <a href="https://www.qd-st.com/press/series-a">inspection technology</a>.</li>
<li><strong>SambaNova</strong> raised $1B to expand capacity for its on-prem <a href="https://sambanova.ai/press/sambanova-completes-first-close-of-1b-financing-at-11b-valuation">agentic inference chips</a> that use a dataflow architecture.</li>
<li><strong>Oratomic</strong> raised $300M to build a fault-tolerant quantum computer based on reconfigurable <a href="https://www.oratomic.com/news/SeriesA">atomic arrays</a> trapped in focused laser beams.</li>
<li><strong>SK hynix</strong> began <a href="https://news.skhynix.com/skhynix-lists-adrs-on-nasdaq/">trading on Nasdaq</a> through American depositary receipts. The offering initially raised ~$26.5B, providing additional capital for manufacturing expansion and strengthening the company’s presence in U.S. capital markets.</li>
</ul>
<h3 role="heading" aria-level="2" data-uw-rm-heading="level"><strong>New analysis</strong></h3>
<ul>
<li role="heading" aria-level="2" data-uw-rm-heading="level"><strong>SIA/WSTS</strong> reports <a href="https://www.semiconductors.org/global-semiconductor-sales-increase-9-2-month-to-month-in-may/">global semiconductor sales</a> hit a record ~$121B in May 2026, up over 9% from April and over 104% from May 2025.</li>
<li role="heading" aria-level="2" data-uw-rm-heading="level">A <strong>NNME/SEMI/NSF/McKinsey</strong> analysis found the U.S. semiconductor industry will <a href="https://nnme.org/new-national-analysis-highlights-opportunity-to-strengthen-americas-semiconductor-workforce-through-coordinated-action/">need 189K additional workers</a> by 2030, with 74% of the projected demand related to manufacturing. The report noted that 75% of surveyed employers had difficulty hiring engineers.</li>
</ul>
<h3><strong>AI </strong></h3>
<ul>
<li><strong>Meta</strong>’s in-house <a href="https://www.reuters.com/world/asia-pacific/meta-put-ai-chip-into-production-september-it-looks-double-computing-capacity-2026-07-09/">AI accelerator is moving toward manufacturing</a>, with a goal of doubling compute capacity and reducing reliance on outside AI-chip suppliers, reports Reuters.</li>
<li><strong>TetraMem</strong> and <strong>SK hynix</strong> demonstrated progress on <a href="https://www.businesswire.com/news/home/20260708244132/en/TetraMem-and-SK-hynix-Showcase-Successful-Technology-Collaboration-Advancing-Memory-Centric-AI-Computing?">analog in-memory computing for AI</a>, using resistive memory to process data closer to where it is stored.</li>
<li><strong>Flex</strong> and <strong>Cerebras</strong> expanded U.S. production of Cerebras&#8217; <a href="https://investors.flex.com/news/news-details/2026/Flex-and-Cerebras-Expand-Partnership-to-Scale-American-Manufacturing-of-Cerebras-AI-Supercomputers/default.aspx">AI supercomputers</a>, supporting an expected 7X production ramp for wafer-scale AI systems through 2026. Cerebras also said it will bring its <a href="https://investors.cerebras.ai/news-releases/news-release-details/cerebras-systems-accelerates-european-expansion-200mw-ai-compute">first European AI data-center capacity</a> online by the end of this year.</li>
<li>The <strong>Economist</strong> tested <a href="https://www.economist.com/briefing/2026/06/25/ai-models-values-are-very-different-from-most-peoples">25 leading AI models</a> on morals and beliefs, finding that many don’t reflect the values held by most people around the world. That gap matters most when AI is used for judgment calls — in areas like advice, policy, education, or hiring — where a model’s built-in assumptions can shape the answer without users realizing it.</li>
</ul>
<h3><strong>Legal matters</strong></h3>
<ul>
<li>The U.S. ITC reaffirmed a previous patent finding that<strong> Innoscience</strong> <a href="https://www.infineon.com/press-release/2026/ifxpr202607-118">infringed on an Infineon patent</a> concerning GaN technology, resulting in import and sales bans against the Chinese semiconductor developer.</li>
<li><strong>Wolfspeed</strong> filed a <a href="https://www.wolfspeed.com/company/news-events/news/wolfspeed-files-patent-infringement-lawsuit-against-navitas-semiconductor/">patent-infringement lawsuit</a> in Delaware, alleging <strong>Navitas&#8217;</strong> GaN-based FETs and other products infringe on multiple Wolfspeed patents. Navitas <a href="https://navitassemi.com/navitas-semiconductor-responds-to-patent-infringement-complaint-filed-by-wolfspeed/">disputes</a> the allegations.</li>
</ul>
<h3><strong>Around the Valley</strong></h3>
<p><img data-recalc-dims="1" fetchpriority="high" decoding="async" class="alignnone wp-image-24278762" src="https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Screenshot-2026-07-09-at-4.45.40-PM.png?resize=430%2C275&#038;ssl=1" alt="" width="430" height="275" srcset="https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Screenshot-2026-07-09-at-4.45.40-PM.png?w=1008&amp;ssl=1 1008w, https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Screenshot-2026-07-09-at-4.45.40-PM.png?resize=300%2C192&amp;ssl=1 300w, https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Screenshot-2026-07-09-at-4.45.40-PM.png?resize=768%2C491&amp;ssl=1 768w, https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Screenshot-2026-07-09-at-4.45.40-PM.png?resize=600%2C383&amp;ssl=1 600w" sizes="(max-width: 430px) 100vw, 430px" /><br />
<strong>Amkor SVP David McCann (L) and ASE VP of R&amp;D CP Hung (R) discuss future challenges and directions for advanced packaging at <a href="https://imaps.org/page/IMAPS-Chipcon">iMAPS CHIPCon</a> conference in Santa Clara, California.</strong></p>

<hr />
<h2 id="Global"><strong>Global</strong></h2>
<h3><strong>Europe</strong></h3>
<ul>
<li>The EU&#8217;s <strong>Chips JU</strong> opened its latest <a href="https://www.chips-ju.europa.eu/NewsDetails/?id=2e25dc95-c37a-f111-ab0f-7ced8d726cac">round</a> of applications for projects that aim to advance research, infrastructure, power electronics, photonics, health, 6G, and international cooperation.</li>
<li><strong>Harvard&#8217;s Belfer Center</strong> said the <a href="https://www.belfercenter.org/research-analysis/eu-tech-sovereignty-package-policy-brief">EU’s proposed Tech Sovereignty Package</a> — including Chips Act 2.0 and the Cloud/AI Development Act — marks a move from only subsidizing European fabs toward building demand for advanced chips across strategic sectors.  The brief says the effort is more likely to strengthen niche AI-chip design and ecosystem capabilities than to dramatically raise Europe’s share of chip manufacturing.</li>
<li><strong>POLYN Technology</strong> and <strong>Alter Technology</strong> plan to establish a joint <a href="https://polyn.ai/polyn-technology-and-alter-technology-france-establish-a-joint-chip-validation-lab/">chip-validation lab</a> in Toulouse, France, with specialized electrical validation capabilities for neuromorphic analog signal-processing chips.</li>
</ul>
<h3><strong>Asia</strong></h3>
<ul>
<li><strong>Asahi Kasei</strong> built a SUNFORT <a href="https://www.asahi-kasei.com/news/2026/e260703?">dry-film photoresist slitting facility</a> in <strong>Taiwan</strong>.</li>
<li><strong>Rigaku</strong> opened a dedicated training f<a href="https://www.businesswire.com/news/home/20260708402770/en/Rigaku-Opens-Rigaku-Solutions-Center-Osaka-to-Strengthen-Global-Semiconductor-Metrology-Service-Capabilities">acility for semiconductor metrology</a> systems in Osaka, <strong>Japan</strong>.</li>
<li>In <strong>India, CG Semi </strong>began commercial production at its G1 <a href="https://www.businesswire.com/news/home/20260704038873/en/CG-Semi-Commences-Commercial-Production-at-Its-G1-OSAT-Facility-in-Sanand-Gujarat">OSAT facility</a> in Sanand, Gujarat.</li>
</ul>
<h3><strong>Americas</strong></h3>
<ul>
<li><strong>Carnegie Mellon</strong> and the <strong>U. of Pittsburgh</strong> will build a new <a href="https://www.cmu.edu/news/stories/archives/2026/july/nsf-awards-10m-for-next-generation-supercomputer-at-pittsburgh-supercomputing-center">supercomputer</a> with $10M backing from the U.S. <strong>NSF</strong>.</li>
</ul>

<h2 id="Reports and Deals"><strong>Reports and Deals</strong></h2>
<h3><strong>More deals, launches</strong></h3>
<ul>
<li><strong>Arteris&#8217;</strong> <a href="https://www.arteris.com/press-releases/arteris-announces-collaboration-with-ic-link-by-imec-to-accelerate-next-gen-ai-and-hpc-silicon/">NoC IP</a> will be integrated into a high-speed I/O subsystem reference design for AI and HPC ASICs from IC-Link by <strong>imec</strong>.</li>
<li><strong>Swissbit</strong> and <strong>Nexperia</strong> are collaborating on <a href="https://www.nexperia.com/about/news-events/press-releases/Swissbit-and-Nexperia-collaborate-to-support-AI-cloud-and-server-platforms">secure, high-reliability hardware for AI, cloud, and server</a> platforms.</li>
<li><strong>ADI</strong> completed its <a href="https://www.analog.com/en/newsroom/press-releases/2026/5-19-2026-adi-to-acquire-empower-semiconductor.html">$1.5B acquisition</a> of integrated voltage regulator maker <strong>Empower Semi</strong>.</li>
</ul>
<h3><strong>Fundings, IPO</strong></h3>
<ul>
<li><strong>e-peas</strong> raised $22M to expand its line of <a href="https://e-peas.com/press-releases/e-peas-secures-usd-22-million-in-financing-to-reinforce-its-leading-position-within-the-ambient-iot-market/">energy harvesting</a> power management chips that enable battery-free or long‑lifetime IoT devices.</li>
<li><strong>Pixel-Flo</strong> raised ~$7M to industrialize its fluidic self-assembly technology for <a href="https://pixel-flo.com/news/pixel-flo-raises-seed-round">MicroLED</a> display manufacturing.</li>
<li><strong>Syntiant</strong>, a provider of processors, sensors, and software for physical AI, <a href="https://www.syntiant.com/press-release/syntiant-publicly-files-registration-statement-for-proposed-initial-public-offering/">filed for an IPO</a> on Nasdaq.</li>
<li><strong>CCRAFT</strong> raised $7.8M to expand its <a href="https://www.ccraft.com/_files/ugd/13c319_1748ac20e85c4e3499847ba568b32221.pdf">thin-film lithium niobate</a> (TFLN) foundry.</li>
</ul>

<hr />
<h2 id="New Technologies"><strong>New Technologies</strong></h2>
<p><strong>Accellera</strong> released a <a href="https://www.accellera.org/images/downloads/drafts-review/FSL-1.0-draft.pdf">draft version</a> of the upcoming <a href="https://www.accellera.org/activities/working-groups/functional-safety">Functional Safety Language</a> standard for <a href="https://forums.accellera.org/topic/8210-functional-safety-draft-standard-available-for-public-review/">public review</a> through August 14. FSL defines the syntax and semantics of a language and data model for representing and exchanging functional safety intent.</p>
<p><strong>Rambus</strong> introduced a DDR5 server <a href="https://www.rambus.com/blogs/enabling-next-generation-data-center-infrastructure-for-agentic-ai-introducing-the-rambus-ddr5-9600-server-rdimm-chipset/">RDIMM chipset</a> operating at up to 9600 MT/s for AI infrastructure and HPC.</p>
<p><strong>Samsung Electronics</strong> started mass production of a 4-16TB PCIe 6.0 <a href="https://semiconductor.samsung.com/news-events/news/samsung-begins-mass-production-of-pm1763-ssd-optimized-for-next-generation-ai-infrastructure/">SSD</a> for AI and HPC server environments.</p>
<p><strong>Infineon</strong> will <a href="https://www.infineon.com/technology-news/2026/ifxtn202607-120">supply</a> 1,200 V CoolSiC MOSFETs and matching dual-channel EiceDRIVER 2EDB9259Y gate drivers to <strong>Advantics</strong> for its new line of liquid-cooled power converters.</p>
<p><strong>Keysight</strong> updated its software test tool to find <a href="https://www.keysight.com/us/en/about/newsroom/news-releases/2026/0708_PR26-086-keysight-targets-the-hidden-cost-of-ui-test-authoring-and-maintenance.html">UI elements</a> by description rather than visual appearance.</p>
<p><strong>Daikin</strong> and <strong>NTT DATA</strong> launched a proof of concept for <a href="https://www.daikin.com/press/2026/20260706">AI-based data-center cooling optimization</a>, combining server thermal prediction with integrated control of HVAC, chillers, and liquid cooling for AI server environments.</p>
<hr />
<h2 id="Research"><strong>Research</strong></h2>
<p><strong>MITRE, UC</strong> <strong>Boulder</strong>, and other researchers built <a href="https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01514">piezo-optomechanical photonic circuits</a> directly on commercial CMOS driver wafers, creating a monolithic platform for dense electronic control of silicon nitride photonics used in AI, sensing, and quantum-computing systems.</p>
<p>Using the optical Kerr effect, a <strong>Caltech</strong> team demonstrated &#8220;ultrafast, reconfigurable <a href="https://www.caltech.edu/about/news/steering-light-in-a-flash-new-chip-redirects-light-beams-in-less-than-a-trillionth-of-a-second">all-optical beam steering and spatial light modulation</a>.&#8221;</p>

<h2 id="Security"><strong>Security</strong></h2>
<h3><strong>New security offerings</strong></h3>
<ul>
<li><strong>Infineon</strong> rolled out SECORA ID Key S USB, a Java Card-based solution with <a href="https://www.infineon.com/technology-news/2026/ifxtn202607-119">USB and NFC connectivity for secure authentication and digital signatures</a>.</li>
<li><strong>Keysight</strong> announced the APS-ONE-400, a new <a href="https://www.keysight.com/us/en/about/newsroom/news-releases/2026/0709_pr26-083-keysight-delivers-new-high-performance-4x100ge-network-cybersecurity-test-platform.html">modular network cybersecurity test platform</a>. This new 4x100GE platform increases performance for Layer 4-7 traffic, encrypted traffic, and Elephant Flow traffic, all within a 1-rack unit system.</li>
</ul>
<h3><strong>Government on cybersecurity</strong></h3>
<ul>
<li>The <strong>U.S. Department of Defense</strong> opened applications for its new cyber apprenticeship program after <a href="https://www.war.gov/News/Releases/Release/Article/4533748/launch-your-cyber-career-department-of-war-cyber-apprenticeship-applications-no/">receiving more than 70,000 applicant inquiries</a> since the program was first teased in April.</li>
<li>The <strong>European Commission</strong> <a href="https://commission.europa.eu/news-and-media/news/new-eu-plan-address-risks-and-opportunities-advanced-ai-cybersecurity-2026-07-07_en">published a new plan for AI security</a>, setting new standards for AI evaluation, research, and cyber defense best practices.</li>
<li>The government of Alberta, <strong>Canada</strong> has been using Claude <a href="https://www.anthropic.com/news/alberta-government-claude-cybersecurity">to test for cybersecurity vulnerabilities</a>, with the province’s Ministry of Technology and Innovation publishing <a href="https://thevelocitywhitepapers.com/">a series of whitepapers</a> on their findings.</li>
<li>The <strong>UK</strong> government published a frontier-AI defense plan called <a href="https://www.ncsc.gov.uk/blogs/cyber-shield-the-path-to-an-agentic-ai-future-for-cyber-defence">Cyber Shield</a>, calling on organizations to ensure rapid patching of vulnerabilities, reduce reliance on legacy systems, and adopt secure-by-design technologies.</li>
</ul>
<h3><strong>Warnings, vulnerabilities</strong></h3>
<ul>
<li><strong>CISA</strong> published a post-mortem on<a href="https://www.cisa.gov/news-events/news/lessons-cisas-cyber-incident"> lessons from its own cyber incident</a>, warning organizations should restrict who can upload code or files to public repositories, monitor repositories for exposed secrets, and ensure exposed credentials can be quickly detected, revoked, and replaced.  The cyber agency also posted <a href="https://www.cisa.gov/news-events/cybersecurity-advisories">new vulnerability alerts</a> this week.</li>
<li><strong>CERT/CC’s</strong> latest vulnerability note include a <a href="https://kb.cert.org/vuls/id/213560">Tenda firmware backdoor</a> and a HP Deskjet webserver <a href="https://kb.cert.org/vuls/id/828543">authorization flaw</a> exposing device and wireless credentials.</li>
<li><strong>Cloudflare</strong> said post-quantum signature <a href="https://blog.cloudflare.com/ml-dsa-will-have-to-do/">migration cannot wait for better algorithms</a>, arguing that ML-DSA’s size and implementation tradeoffs are acceptable for the first transition because newer NIST candidates are unlikely to be widely available before the 2030s.</li>
<li><strong>Cisco</strong> and<strong> Foundry</strong> predict <a href="https://www.cisco.com/c/m/en_us/solutions/networking/ai-impact-campus-branch-networks.html">AI will triple network traffic</a> over the next three years, driving growing concerns over network infrastructure and security risks.</li>
</ul>
<h3><strong>Security research</strong></h3>
<ul>
<li>Agentic Conversational AI Assistant for<a href="https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01668"> HW Security Verification</a> (<strong>UF</strong>)</li>
<li>Security &amp; Privacy Framework for<a href="https://arxiv.org/html/2607.01019"> AI-Native 6G Networks</a> (<strong>U Of York</strong>)</li>
<li><a href="https://www.usenix.org/conference/usenixsecurity26/presentation/zhang-guoming">EM Covert Channels</a> via Imperceptible Pixel Modulation (<strong>Shandong</strong> et al)</li>
<li><a href="https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04055">DL HW: Survey of Side-Channel</a> Vulnerabilities/Countermeasures (<strong>Tehran</strong>)</li>
<li>Kill-Chain Attack on Quantum NNs: <a href="https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03337">Demo on Trapped-Ion HW</a>  (<strong>d-fine, AISEC</strong>)</li>
</ul>
<hr />
<h2 id="Vehicles, Batteries"><strong>Vehicles, Batteries</strong></h2>
<h3><strong>Autonomous, connectivity, drones</strong></h3>
<ul>
<li>The <strong>U.S. NHTSA</strong> issued a warning to AV developers after federal officials <a href="https://www.nhtsa.gov/press-releases/av-developers-automated-vehicle-that-cannot-safely-interact-first-responders-danger">identified a pattern of driverless vehicles “interfering”</a> with first responders at emergency scenes.</li>
<li><strong>Tesla</strong> launched its <a href="https://x.com/robotaxi/status/2073030246161367153?s=20">robotaxi service</a> for customers in Miami. <strong>Waymo&#8217;s </strong>AVs will now be available in <a href="https://waymo.com/blog/shorts/ro-den-lv-sd-tmpa/">San Diego, Denver, Las Vegas, and Tampa</a>.</li>
<li><strong>Molex</strong> announced its new <a href="https://www.molex.com/en-us/news/molex-delivers-next-generation-hsautolink-g-automotive-ethernet-connector-system-to-address-rising-adas-compute-demands-and-zonal-architectures?">HSAutoLink G Connector System,</a> capable of supporting multi-gig Ethernet connections of up to 25Gbps.</li>
<li><strong>Innoviz Technologies</strong> and <strong>Cogniteam</strong> will jointly develop an <a href="https://www.prnewswire.com/news-releases/innoviz-lidar-powers-cogniteams-new-counter-uas-classification-module-for-advanced-security-platforms-302819412.html">advanced Counter-UAS</a> drone detection perception module.</li>
<li>Irish drone-delivery company <strong>Manna Aero</strong> announced a large-scale U.S. expansion, with <a href="https://www.manna.aero/blog/manna-announces-major-us-expansion">a new manufacturing and business operations</a> base planned for Tulsa, OK.</li>
</ul>
<h3><strong>EVs</strong></h3>
<ul>
<li>Nearly a year after federal EV credits were terminated, first-time EV buyers in California can now get an <a href="https://www.nbclosangeles.com/news/local/first-time-ev-buyers-in-ca-can-get-3500-rebate-but-theres-a-catch/3913661/">instant $3,500 rebate</a> for new cars under $50k.</li>
<li><strong>Fiat</strong> introduced a tiny all-electric Topolino to the U.S. market, pricing the two-seat neighborhood <a href="https://www.cnbc.com/2026/07/07/stellantis-fiat-topolino-ev.html">EV starting at $13,995</a>. However, it can only go 19-25 mph, has a maximum range of just 46 miles, and functions more like a golf cart.</li>
<li><strong>FORVIA HELLA</strong> launched a <a href="https://www.forvia.com/en/press/forvia-hella-launches-its-first-12-volt-lithium-ion-battery-pack">12V lithium-ion battery pack</a> that integrates cells and a low-voltage BMS for EVs.</li>
</ul>
<h3><strong>Vehicle research</strong></h3>
<ul>
<li>Evaluating <a href="https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00039">HW Abstraction Layer Concepts</a> for SDVs (<strong>Stuttgart</strong>)</li>
<li><a href="https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.07226">Monitoring Vulnerabilities</a> in Next-Gen Automotive OS (<strong>IP Paris</strong>)</li>
<li><a href="https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.05227">AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform</a> from an Industry Perspective (<strong>Chalmers</strong>)</li>
<li><a href="https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.02300">Testing of Vision Language Models</a> for Scene Understanding (<strong>BMW, TUM</strong>)</li>
<li>HW Rooted Authentication for <a href="https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.07371">Secure OTA Updates in Zonal SDVs</a> (<strong>Inria</strong> et al.)</li>
<li><a href="https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.04587">Fusion of Camera, LiDAR, 4D RADAR</a> for Robust 3D Object Detection in Adverse Weather (<strong>KAIST</strong>)</li>
</ul>

<h2 id="Trending Video"><strong>Trending Video</strong></h2>
<p><a href="https://semiengineering.com/new-usb-standards-benefits-and-incompatibilities/">New USB Standards: Benefits And Incompatibilities</a>: A roadmap for integrating different versions of the USB and eUSB.</p>
<p>Just because it’s a standard doesn’t mean everything will work together, and this is especially evident with conflicting USB standards. Cadence&#8217;s <strong>David Shin </strong>explains where incompatibilities can crop up, why it’s so difficult to control the different versions, and what’s behind all this confusion.<br />
<iframe src="https://www.youtube.com/embed/ROxK6uNQ2qk" width="480" height="270" frameborder="0" allowfullscreen="allowfullscreen"></iframe></p>

<h2 id="Quantum"><strong>Quantum</strong></h2>
<p>Quantum technology is at a stage where <a href="https://semiengineering.com/where-does-quantum-computing-stand/">maximum creativity meets maximum chaos</a>. The industry hasn’t settled on a common approach to quantum computing, so different research groups have been working on different implementations simultaneously. Numerous small startups have sprung up, each addressing some aspect of a quantum system.</p>
<p><strong>IQM Quantum Computers </strong>was <a href="https://iqm.tech/press-releases/iqm-quantum-computers-becomes-first-european-quantum-computing-company-listed-on-a-major-u-s-exchange/">listed</a> on Nasdaq following the completion of a SPAC deal. IQM also <a href="https://iqm.tech/press-releases/iqm-quantum-computers-acquires-assets-of-quantistry-gmbh-to-bridge-the-gap-between-quantum-algorithms-and-solutions-for-industrial-enterprises/">acquired</a> <strong>Quantistry</strong>, a provider of AI-powered quantum chemistry and materials simulation software.</p>
<p><strong>QpiAI</strong> open-sourced its <a href="https://www.qpiai.tech/pressreleases/2026-07-07-qpiai-open-sources-quantum-sdk">SDK</a>, which provides a Python-based interface to build and run quantum algorithms.</p>
<h3><strong>Quantum research</strong></h3>
<ul>
<li><strong>ETH Zurich</strong> researchers designed <a href="https://ethz.ch/en/news-and-events/eth-news/news/2026/07/inside-this-computer-chip-the-memory-vibrates.html">quantum memory</a> using mechanical resonators that vibrate when storing information.</li>
<li><strong>Oak Ridge National Laboratory</strong>, <strong>Cleveland Clinic</strong>, and <strong>IBM</strong> used a quantum computer to calculate nine <a href="https://newsroom.ibm.com/2026-07-06-oak-ridge-national-lab,-cleveland-clinic,-and-ibm-achieve-first-known-computations-of-fusion-materials-on-a-quantum-computer">molecular configurations</a> of a potential fusion energy material.</li>
</ul>
<h3><strong>Government on quantum</strong></h3>
<ul>
<li>The U.S. <strong>NSF</strong> launched an initiative to <a href="https://www.nsf.gov/news/nsf-launches-project-triad-advance-quantum-technology-real">integrate</a> quantum sensing, quantum networking, and quantum computing into a single operational system.</li>
<li><strong>Illinois</strong> will <a href="https://www.illinois.gov/news/release.html?releaseid=32699">offer $3M</a> to encourage NSF X-Labs quantum research teams to set up in the state.</li>
</ul>
<hr />
<h2 id="Workforce, Education"><strong>Workforce, Education</strong></h2>
<p><strong>ITIF</strong> argues that the US&#8217;s <a href="https://itif.org/publications/2026/07/01/visa-barriers-are-undermining-us-industrial-competitiveness/">outdated VISA categories are undermining US competitiveness</a> and recommends a new visa category for foreign technical experts, researchers, and trainers whose work supports U.S. market leadership.</p>
<p><strong>Purdue</strong> expanded its semiconductor professional education portfolio with Taiwan’s <strong>NYCU</strong>, adding <a href="https://www.purdue.edu/newsroom/2026/Q3/purdue-expands-semiconductor-professional-education-portfolio-to-advance-global-workforce-development/">joint stackable industry courses</a> in AI chip design, packaging, reliability, characterization, and more.</p>
<p><strong>SEMI</strong> warns that <a href="https://www.semi.org/en/blogs/powering-the-semiconductor-construction-boom-through-workforce-collaboration">skilled trades are becoming a big roadblock</a> on the IC construction buildout, with large fab projects requiring thousands of workers and more specialized training in cleanroom construction, electrical systems, safety, and contamination control.</p>
<p>New York&#8217;s <strong>Binghamton University</strong> opened a <a href="https://www.binghamton.edu/news/story/6346/binghamton-cleanroom-research-facility--advanced-chip-manufacturing-upstate-ny-jobs">new cleanroom and microelectronic packaging research facility</a> to train more than 100 students a year in semiconductor manufacturing and packaging skills.</p>
<div><strong>Florida&#8217;s St. Petersburg College</strong> received <strong data-start="1087" data-end="1101">$1M </strong>in federal funding for its <a href="https://stpetecatalyst.com/luna-delivers-1m-to-spc-to-strengthen-workforce-pipeline/">SMART Tech Industry 4.0 Lab</a>, which will support semiconductor, mechatronics, automation, and robotics technician training.</div>
<hr />

<li><a class="e-title" href="https://developer.arm.com/embedded-and-microcontrollers/modelnova-fusion-studio-with-keil-mdk" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer" data-uw-rm-brl="PR" data-uw-original-href="https://developer.arm.com/embedded-and-microcontrollers/modelnova-fusion-studio-with-keil-mdk" aria-label="Integrating ModelNova Fusion Studio with Arm Keil MDK - open in a new tab" data-uw-rm-ext-link="">Integrating ModelNova Fusion Studio</a> with Arm Keil MDK, July 21</li>
<li><a class="e-title" href="https://riscure.ac-page.com/attacking-ai-with-fi-advancing-fi-with-ai-assistance" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer" data-uw-rm-brl="PR" data-uw-original-href="https://riscure.ac-page.com/attacking-ai-with-fi-advancing-fi-with-ai-assistance" aria-label="Attacking AI with Fault Injection, Advancing FI with AI Assistance - open in a new tab" data-uw-rm-ext-link="">Attacking AI with Fault Injection</a>, Advancing FI with AI Assistance, July 21</li>

		<post-id xmlns="com-wordpress:feed-additions:1">24278474</post-id>	</item>
		<item>
		<title>The Architecture Decisions Behind A Production-Ready EDA AI Agent</title>
		<link>https://semiengineering.com/the-architecture-decisions-behind-a-production-ready-eda-ai-agent/</link>
					<comments>https://semiengineering.com/the-architecture-decisions-behind-a-production-ready-eda-ai-agent/#respond</comments>
		
		<dc:creator><![CDATA[Emma-Jane Crozier]]></dc:creator>
		<pubDate>Thu, 09 Jul 2026 07:08:30 +0000</pubDate>
				<category><![CDATA[Best Of Both: LP & HP]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[Blogs - LPHP]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[Low Power-High Performance]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[agentic AI]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[AI]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[AI agents]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[AI in EDA]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[data management]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[domain grounding]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[EDA]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[LLMs]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[MCP]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[Model Context Protocol]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[PCBs]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[RAG]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[retrieval-augmented generation]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[Siemens EDA]]></category>
		<guid isPermaLink="false">https://semiengineering.com/?p=24278606</guid>

					<description><![CDATA[<p>Building an AI agent that works in semiconductor and PCB design requires solving problems that generic agentic frameworks were never designed to handle. </p>
<p>The post <a href="https://semiengineering.com/the-architecture-decisions-behind-a-production-ready-eda-ai-agent/">The Architecture Decisions Behind A Production-Ready EDA AI Agent</a> appeared first on <a href="https://semiengineering.com">Semiconductor Engineering</a>.</p>
]]></description>
										<content:encoded><![CDATA[<p>The conversation about agentic AI in semiconductor and PCB design tends to focus on capability: what the agent can do, how much time it saves, and which parts of the workflow it can automate. That is a reasonable place to start, but that is not where the hard engineering happens. Organizations are now asking whether AI agents can take on meaningful portions of the workflow, not as assistants that answer questions, but as systems that execute tasks autonomously. The answer is increasingly yes, but only if the agent was built for the environment it is being asked to operate in.</p>
<p>The hard engineering happens at the architecture level, in the decisions that determine whether an agent can operate in a production EDA environment rather than a controlled demonstration. These decisions are not solved by taking a general-purpose agentic framework and pointing it at EDA tools. They require deliberate architectural choices, each one motivated by a specific constraint that chip and PCB design imposes.</p>
<h2>Why generic AI knowledge fails in EDA</h2>
<p>Generic large language models are trained on publicly available text. That corpus does not contain the configuration requirements of specialized EDA tools, the sequencing logic that governs multi-tool workflows, or the production methodology patterns that experienced engineers have developed over years of practice. A generic model can approximate the basics, but errors emerge at execution time, where they are most expensive to catch.</p>
<p>The architectural response begins with a centralized, multimodal EDA data lake that breaks down team and tool silos, ensuring every workflow operates from a single source of truth. Layered on top is a custom-built retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) framework that is optimized for Siemens EDA tools and methodologies, enabling precise answers to complex domain-specific queries. This is complemented by Agent Skills: executable playbooks that complete complex, multi-step tasks across EDA workflows by integrating domain-specific knowledge, with built-in validation and guardrails at each step.</p>
<h2>Infrastructure compatibility without data movement</h2>
<p>EDA environments are not cloud-native. The reasons are practical: design data is sensitive IP, verification jobs run for hours or days on high-performance computing clusters, and datasets are measured in terabytes. The infrastructure that supports this is typically on premises, integrated with job schedulers tuned over years to manage resource allocation across large compute environments.</p>
<p>Building for production means an agent must manage long-running verification jobs without losing state, integrate with existing job scheduling frameworks rather than replacing them, and operate on terabyte-scale datasets in place rather than requiring data movement. It also needs to be designed to function across both on-premises and cloud environments, giving organizations the deployment flexibility their infrastructure and security requirements demand. A centralized installation across the EDA workflow means distributed data can be shared seamlessly, without requiring each tool or environment to manage its own access independently.</p>
<p>These are the standard operating conditions of production EDA environments, not edge cases.</p>
<h2>Scalable orchestration across a fragmented tool ecosystem</h2>
<p>A production EDA workflow spans dozens of specialized systems covering RTL design, functional verification, place-and-route, physical sign-off, and PCB system design, sourced from one or multiple vendors, operating on different data formats. Orchestrating across that ecosystem is the central challenge.</p>
<p>The specific failure mode for generic AI systems here is context saturation. As the number of tools grows, the information required to orchestrate coherently exceeds what a single context window can hold. The result is degraded reasoning quality, inconsistent tool sequencing, and hallucinated outputs that appear correct but are not.</p>
<p>The architectural response is a unified orchestration layer built on a Model Context Protocol (MCP) foundation, which centralizes tool discovery and prevents context saturation as workflows expand. MCP enables dynamic tool discovery and orchestration across connected EDA tools, keeping the system&#8217;s operational scope manageable while allowing it to coordinate across an arbitrarily complex tool ecosystem.</p>
<p>The modularity works like this: each individual sub-flow is automated in detail and functions as a self-contained unit, built from Agent Skills. Once a library of these automated sub-flows exists, they can be strung together to construct comprehensive, multi-tool workflows that span the entire EDA lifecycle. The orchestration does not need to be redesigned as the workflow grows since it is naturally designed to scale.</p>
<p>This modularity also makes the orchestration layer genuinely multi-vendor and model-agnostic. EDA workflows cannot be confined to a single vendor&#8217;s ecosystem, and an architecture that assumes otherwise will fail at the first organization whose workflow spans more than one vendor&#8217;s tools.</p>
<h2>Native interpretation of EDA data formats</h2>
<p>EDA data is not text. Netlists, layouts, waveforms, and design rule check outputs exist in dense binary formats and proprietary database structures. An agent that cannot read native EDA formats is not operating on the actual data. It is operating on a pre-processed summary of that data, and its reasoning is only as good as that summary.</p>
<p>The architectural decision is to build domain-specific parsers capable of extracting precise, actionable context from the actual artifacts EDA workflows produce: LEF/DEF files, GDSII, waveform databases, and other format-specific outputs. These parsers feed directly into the EDA data lake, ensuring that intelligence extracted from raw design artifacts is available as a shared, consistent resource across every tool and workflow stage.</p>
<h2>Security and governance at the execution layer</h2>
<p>The real IP risk is what the agent does once it is running: whether it can access data outside its intended scope, whether its actions are logged in a way that supports audit and review, and whether there are key checkpoints at which engineers can approve consequential decisions before they execute.</p>
<p>The architectural decision is to embed security and governance at the execution layer. Role-based, customizable access controls govern what the agent can do, ensuring each team member only has visibility into the data they are authorized to view. Strict sandboxing ensures the agent operates within defined boundaries, preventing access to data or systems outside its intended scope. Comprehensive audit trails record what the agent did and why. Human-in-the-loop checkpoints are built into the workflow at decision points where the consequences of an error warrant review before execution proceeds.</p>
<p>An agent that operates transparently, within defined boundaries, and with clear mechanisms for human oversight is not a less capable agent. It is a more trustworthy one, and in a high-stakes engineering environment, that is the prerequisite for expanded autonomy.</p>
<h2>Why these decisions compound</h2>
<p>These architectural decisions are not independent. Domain grounding without infrastructure compatibility produces an agent that reasons correctly but cannot operate where the reasoning is needed. Infrastructure compatibility without native data parsing produces an agent that runs in the right place but makes decisions from incomplete information. Scalable orchestration without domain grounding produces an agent that coordinates across a complex tool ecosystem but cannot execute reliably within any individual tool.</p>
<p>An organization evaluating agentic AI for EDA should be asking exactly these questions: Where does the agent get its domain knowledge? How does it handle the infrastructure it will actually run on? How does it scale as workflows grow? What does it do with data it cannot natively read?</p>
<p>For EDA AI agents to mature from reactive task executors into long-term autonomous partners, they must deliver results engineers can trust and verify at every stage of the design process. That is exactly what Siemens EDA has built with the Fuse EDA AI Agent. To see it in action, visit the <a href="https://www.siemens.com/en-us/products/fuse-eda-ai-system/agent/">Fuse EDA AI Agent product page</a>.</p>
<p>The post <a href="https://semiengineering.com/the-architecture-decisions-behind-a-production-ready-eda-ai-agent/">The Architecture Decisions Behind A Production-Ready EDA AI Agent</a> appeared first on <a href="https://semiengineering.com">Semiconductor Engineering</a>.</p>
]]></content:encoded>

			<slash:comments>0</slash:comments>
		
		
		<post-id xmlns="com-wordpress:feed-additions:1">24278606</post-id>	</item>
		<item>
		<title>The Expansion Of LPDDR Into Edge AI Platforms</title>
		<link>https://semiengineering.com/the-expansion-of-lpddr-into-edge-ai-platforms/</link>
					<comments>https://semiengineering.com/the-expansion-of-lpddr-into-edge-ai-platforms/#respond</comments>
		
		<dc:creator><![CDATA[Paul Karazuba]]></dc:creator>
		<pubDate>Thu, 09 Jul 2026 07:07:13 +0000</pubDate>
				<category><![CDATA[A Bit About Memory]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[Blogs - LPHP]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[Low Power-High Performance]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[AI]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[automotive]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[edge AI]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[LPDDR]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[memory]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[Rambus]]></category>
		<guid isPermaLink="false">https://semiengineering.com/?p=24278620</guid>

					<description><![CDATA[<p>Characteristics once primarily associated with extending smartphone battery life map directly onto the needs of real-time on-device inference.</p>
<p>The post <a href="https://semiengineering.com/the-expansion-of-lpddr-into-edge-ai-platforms/">The Expansion Of LPDDR Into Edge AI Platforms</a> appeared first on <a href="https://semiengineering.com">Semiconductor Engineering</a>.</p>
]]></description>
										<content:encoded><![CDATA[<p>As artificial intelligence continues its migration from centralized data centers to distributed systems, one reality is becoming unmistakable: the future of AI is increasingly defined at the edge. Whether embedded in smart cameras, industrial controllers, or next-generation vehicles, AI is no longer confined to racks of GPUs. It is operating in power-constrained, thermally limited, and space-restricted environments where system design priorities are fundamentally different. In this new landscape, memory has emerged as a first-order design constraint. And with that shift, LPDDR is rapidly expanding beyond its mobile roots to become a foundational technology for edge and automotive AI platforms.</p>
<p>The underlying driver is simple but profound. AI workloads, particularly inference, are dominated by data movement rather than arithmetic intensity. Moving weights, activations, and intermediate data between compute engines and memory consumes significant energy and directly influences system latency and throughput. In edge environments, where power budgets can be measured in single-digit watts and cooling is minimal or nonexistent, these constraints become decisive. Memory bandwidth, latency, and especially energy per bit transferred increasingly dictate whether a system is viable at all.</p>
<p>This is precisely where LPDDR has found its opportunity. Originally designed for mobile devices, LPDDR is optimized for low I/O voltage, aggressive power management states, and compact packaging. These characteristics, once primarily associated with extending smartphone battery life, map directly onto the needs of edge AI systems. Devices performing real-time inference, from video analytics to industrial automation, must deliver sustained performance without exceeding strict energy and thermal limits. LPDDR provides a balance of bandwidth and efficiency that makes these designs feasible, even as workloads grow in complexity.</p>
<p>The expansion into automotive platforms represents an even more significant inflection point. Modern vehicles are rapidly evolving into software-defined systems, where compute and memory subsystems must manage enormous volumes of sensor data in real time. Advanced driver-assistance systems (ADAS), autonomous driving stacks, and intelligent cockpit experiences all rely on continuous AI inference across multi-modal sensor inputs, including cameras, radar, and lidar. These workloads demand high bandwidth, but they must also operate within tightly constrained thermal envelopes and meet stringent reliability requirements.</p>
<p>LPDDR addresses this convergence of requirements. LPDDR offers lower power consumption, small form factors, and excellent bandwidth efficiency, all of which are critical in automotive environments where heat dissipation, board space, and energy efficiency directly impact system design and cost. As a result, LPDDR5 and LPDDR5X are increasingly deployed in domain controllers, ADAS compute units, and centralized vehicle architectures, where they support real-time sensor fusion and neural network inference without incurring excessive thermal or power penalties.</p>
<p>The shift toward zonal and centralized automotive architectures further amplifies this trend. Rather than distributing compute across dozens of discrete ECUs, next-generation vehicles are consolidating functionality into fewer, more powerful processing nodes. This architectural shift increases the demand for high-capacity, high-bandwidth memory while simultaneously intensifying power density challenges. In such systems, LPDDR’s ability to deliver strong performance per watt becomes a key enabler, allowing designers to scale compute capabilities without exceeding vehicle-level energy budgets or thermal limits.</p>
<p>At the same time, the broader expansion of edge AI is reinforcing the need to optimize memory hierarchies across multiple dimensions. LPDDR occupies a distinct and increasingly important position in the hierarchy of memory solutions, offering sufficient bandwidth for inference workloads while minimizing energy consumption. In effect, it represents the “right-sized” memory solution for a vast and growing segment of AI applications, bridging the gap between ultra-high-performance memory in the cloud and constrained compute at the edge.</p>
<p>Another critical factor driving LPDDR adoption is the emergence of increasingly sophisticated edge AI workloads. As generative AI and agent-based systems move onto local devices, inference patterns are evolving from simple, bursty tasks to more continuous and data-intensive processing pipelines. Even compact models optimized for edge deployment require sustained memory bandwidth to deliver responsive performance. This shift places additional pressure on memory subsystems, elevating the role of LPDDR from a power-optimized alternative to a central determinant of system capability.</p>
<p>In automotive applications, these trends intersect with stringent safety and reliability requirements. Memory subsystems must support functional safety standards, operate across extreme temperature ranges, and maintain deterministic performance under all conditions. LPDDR’s evolution has increasingly incorporated features such as error correction and improved reliability mechanisms, enabling it to meet these demands while preserving its core advantage in energy efficiency. This combination is essential for enabling autonomous and semi-autonomous driving systems, where consistent performance is a safety-critical requirement.</p>
<p>For system designers and architects, the challenge is no longer choosing the fastest memory available. It is selecting the memory that best aligns with application constraints and performance requirements across a complex set of tradeoffs. In this context, LPDDR is emerging as a cornerstone technology; one that enables the next wave of AI innovation not by maximizing peak performance, but by optimizing the balance between performance, power, and integration where it matters most: at the edge and inside the vehicle.</p>
<h3>Link</h3>
<ul>
<li><a href="https://www.rambus.com/interface-ip/lpddr/">Rambus LPDDR Memory Controller IP</a></li>
</ul>
<p>The post <a href="https://semiengineering.com/the-expansion-of-lpddr-into-edge-ai-platforms/">The Expansion Of LPDDR Into Edge AI Platforms</a> appeared first on <a href="https://semiengineering.com">Semiconductor Engineering</a>.</p>
]]></content:encoded>
