		<title>From Host Node To Heterogeneous Rack: Rethinking The AI CPU</title>
		<link>https://semiengineering.com/from-host-node-to-heterogeneous-rack-rethinking-the-ai-cpu/</link>
					<comments>https://semiengineering.com/from-host-node-to-heterogeneous-rack-rethinking-the-ai-cpu/#respond</comments>
		
		<dc:creator><![CDATA[Satadal Bhattacharjee]]></dc:creator>
		<pubDate>Thu, 09 Jul 2026 07:02:11 +0000</pubDate>
				<category><![CDATA[At The Core]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[Blogs - LPHP]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[Low Power-High Performance]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[agentic AI]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[AI]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[AI infrastructure]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[ARM]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[CPUs]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[data centers]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[inference]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[racks]]></category>
		<guid isPermaLink="false">https://semiengineering.com/?p=24278600</guid>

					<description><![CDATA[<p>As AI infrastructure fragments into specialized tiers, CPUs are becoming the orchestration layer for agentic workloads.</p>
<p>The post <a href="https://semiengineering.com/from-host-node-to-heterogeneous-rack-rethinking-the-ai-cpu/">From Host Node To Heterogeneous Rack: Rethinking The AI CPU</a> appeared first on <a href="https://semiengineering.com">Semiconductor Engineering</a>.</p>
]]></description>
										<content:encoded><![CDATA[<p>AI infrastructure is entering a crucial new phase. The first phase of generative AI infrastructure was defined by accelerator scale: how many GPUs, NPUs or custom AI accelerators could be deployed, powered, cooled and connected. That phase is not over, but it is no longer sufficient.</p>
<p>The next phase is about rack-scale system composition: heterogeneous AI racks where different compute resources are optimized for different phases of the agentic AI workflow. Specialized racks of CPUs, accelerators, memory and networking are being assembled into gigawatt-scale AI superclusters, with each rack operating as a dense compute engine for a specific part of the workflow.</p>
<p>As AI inference evolves from single-pass model calls to multi-step agentic pipelines, infrastructure design is shifting from a narrow focus on accelerator scale to a broader question of system composition: how to assemble the right compute for each phase of the agentic AI workflow. Accelerators remain critical for prefill, decode and model execution. CPUs become critical in the surrounding orchestration layer, where agents fan out across tools, APIs, retrieval systems, cloud-native services and multiple model calls.</p>
<p>That shift matters because inference is changing structurally. Traditional inference was often a relatively linear process: a request enters the system, a model generates a response and the transaction ends. <a href="https://newsroom.arm.com/blog/what-is-agentic-ai">Agentic AI</a> behaves differently. Agents plan, retrieve data, call tools, run code, invoke APIs, reason over intermediate results and loop through multiple model calls before producing an answer.</p>
<p><img data-recalc-dims="1" loading="lazy" decoding="async" class="alignnone size-full wp-image-24278602" src="https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Arm_host-node-to-heterogeneous-rack-AI-CPU-fig1.webp?resize=1200%2C412&#038;ssl=1" alt="" width="1200" height="412" srcset="https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Arm_host-node-to-heterogeneous-rack-AI-CPU-fig1.webp?w=1200&amp;ssl=1 1200w, https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Arm_host-node-to-heterogeneous-rack-AI-CPU-fig1.webp?resize=300%2C103&amp;ssl=1 300w, https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Arm_host-node-to-heterogeneous-rack-AI-CPU-fig1.webp?resize=1024%2C352&amp;ssl=1 1024w, https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Arm_host-node-to-heterogeneous-rack-AI-CPU-fig1.webp?resize=768%2C264&amp;ssl=1 768w, https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Arm_host-node-to-heterogeneous-rack-AI-CPU-fig1.webp?resize=600%2C206&amp;ssl=1 600w" sizes="auto, (max-width: 1000px) 100vw, 1000px" /></p>
<p>This changes the shape of the data center. More of the workload sits outside the neural network forward pass. More time is spent coordinating work across accelerators, memory, storage, networking and software services. More infrastructure decisions are made at rack scale rather than server scale.</p>
<p>As <a href="https://www.chipstrat.com/p/are-agentic-cpus-a-commodity-its">Austin Lyons recently argued in Chipstrat</a>, “The CPU is not a commodity, but it is not a single prize either.” That framing is important because heterogeneous AI racks do not need one generic CPU. They need CPUs optimized for the specific work being performed: keeping accelerators fed, managing memory-intensive decode and executing the agentic work that happens between model calls.</p>
<h2>The rack is becoming the AI system</h2>
<p>AI infrastructure is becoming more specialized at each stage of the inference pipeline. One of the clearest examples is the growing separation between prefill and decode. Prefill is the phase where the system processes the input prompt and builds the KV cache; it is typically compute-intensive and accelerator-heavy. Decode is the phase where the system generates the response token by token; as context grows, it becomes increasingly constrained by memory bandwidth, memory capacity and KV cache movement.</p>
<p>A heterogeneous AI rack must make many specialized components behave like one coherent system. It must route requests to the right compute tier, move data efficiently between prefill and decode, manage KV cache transfers, maintain session state, execute non-model work and enforce service-level objectives across the full pipeline.</p>
<p>That is why the industry is moving toward a more system-level view of AI infrastructure. The amount of useful agentic work a data center can perform is not determined by accelerator capacity alone. It also depends on CPU capacity, memory bandwidth, network performance, software orchestration and the ability to balance the whole system under real workload pressure. The architectural question is shifting from “how many accelerators can we deploy?” to “how do we specialize and coordinate each stage of the agentic AI workflow?”</p>
<p>In this architecture, there is not one CPU use case; there are multiple.</p>
<h2>Three CPU roles in the heterogeneous AI rack</h2>
<ul>
<li>The first role is the <strong>prefill host CPU </strong>(coordinates the compute-intensive phase of inference).</li>
<li>The second role is the <strong>decode host CPU</strong> (manages the latency-sensitive, memory-intensive phase of inference).</li>
<li>The third role is the <strong>agent or worker CPU </strong>(the compute tier where the agent lives between model calls).</li>
</ul>
<p>These three roles are related, but they should not be evaluated with the same checklist.</p>
<p>This reframing is important. The industry has often talked about the “host CPU” as a single supporting component. In heterogeneous rack-scale AI, that framing is too narrow. Infrastructure teams need to evaluate CPU capacity according to the distinct work being done at each layer of the inference pipeline.</p>
<h2>Why this changes infrastructure design</h2>
<p>With this new system-level approach, infrastructure teams can right-size each tier based on actual workload behavior. For example, a workload with long prompts and short outputs may require more prefill capacity. A chatbot with long-running conversations may require more decode and memory bandwidth. A multi-agent enterprise workflow may require significantly more worker CPU capacity for tool execution, retrieval, policy checks and application orchestration.</p>
<p>At the same time, one of the historical barriers to heterogeneous infrastructure is beginning to fall. Multi-architecture deployments have traditionally carried real software friction: porting applications, validating software stacks, tuning performance and adapting DevOps workflows across different compute platforms. But agentic software development is compressing that work. AI-assisted porting, testing, validation and operations are reducing the time and risk required to support large-scale multi-architecture environments.</p>
<p>That matters because the architectural question is changing. Infrastructure teams no longer need to force every workload onto a single default architecture simply to avoid operational complexity. They can increasingly choose the right architecture for the right tier of the AI system and use modern software automation to manage the complexity behind the scenes.</p>
<p>This also changes the economics of AI infrastructure. The key question is no longer just which accelerator delivers the highest model throughput. It is how efficiently the full rack converts power, memory, I/O and software coordination into useful AI work.</p>
<p>That is a system question, not a component question.</p>
<p>It is also where Arm’s architectural strengths become especially relevant. AI infrastructure is moving toward heterogeneous compute, high concurrency, power-constrained scaling and software-defined orchestration. These are precisely the conditions where performance per watt, ecosystem breadth and architectural flexibility matter most.</p>
<p><iframe loading="lazy" src="https://www.youtube.com/embed/aURz8CaXTgM" width="480" height="270" frameborder="0" allowfullscreen="allowfullscreen"></iframe><br />
<strong>Mohamed Awad, EVP, Cloud AI Business Unit, Arm, on why performance per watt is the new AI data center benchmark.</strong></p>
<h2>AGI CPU and the agentic infrastructure era</h2>
<p>The <a href="https://newsroom.arm.com/blog/introducing-arm-agi-cpu">Arm AGI CPU </a>was designed for this shift. It extends Arm’s cloud infrastructure roadmap into a new class of agentic AI systems where CPUs play a central role in orchestration, memory coordination, accelerator management and rack-scale efficiency.</p>
<p>For heterogeneous AI racks, the value is not just one specification. It is the combination of high core density, memory bandwidth, high-speed I/O, CXL readiness, software maturity and efficiency within modern datacenter power constraints. Those attributes map directly to the three CPU roles emerging in rack-scale AI I referenced earlier: prefill host, decode host and agent worker.</p>
<p>Arm’s broader ecosystem gives infrastructure builders choice. Arm-based CPUs already sit across cloud, networking, edge and AI systems. Partners can build with Arm IP, Arm Compute Subsystems or production silicon depending on the level of customization and time-to-market they need. That flexibility is becoming more important as AI infrastructure fragments into specialized but interconnected tiers.</p>
<p>The industry is moving beyond a simple accelerator-first view of AI infrastructure. Accelerators remain critical, but they are only one part of the system. The differentiator will increasingly be how well the full rack is orchestrated, how efficiently resources are used and how predictably the system performs under real agentic workloads.</p>
<p>In the agentic AI era, the rack becomes the system. Heterogeneity becomes the norm. And the CPU becomes one of the most important architectural decisions in the AI data center.</p>
<p>The post <a href="https://semiengineering.com/from-host-node-to-heterogeneous-rack-rethinking-the-ai-cpu/">From Host Node To Heterogeneous Rack: Rethinking The AI CPU</a> appeared first on <a href="https://semiengineering.com">Semiconductor Engineering</a>.</p>
]]></content:encoded>

		<post-id xmlns="com-wordpress:feed-additions:1">24278600</post-id>	</item>
		<item>
		<title>Benchmarking An NPU At Scale</title>
		<link>https://semiengineering.com/benchmarking-an-npu-at-scale/</link>
					<comments>https://semiengineering.com/benchmarking-an-npu-at-scale/#respond</comments>
		
		<dc:creator><![CDATA[Marc Meszaros]]></dc:creator>
		<pubDate>Thu, 09 Jul 2026 07:01:39 +0000</pubDate>
				<category><![CDATA[Blogs - LPHP]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[Embedded ML Design]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[Low Power-High Performance]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[AI]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[benchmarking]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[model zoo]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[NPUs]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[Quadric]]></category>
		<guid isPermaLink="false">https://semiengineering.com/?p=24278582</guid>

					<description><![CDATA[<p>Recompiling and re-profiling the entire model zoo across a broad sweep of hardware configurations.</p>
<p>The post <a href="https://semiengineering.com/benchmarking-an-npu-at-scale/">Benchmarking An NPU At Scale</a> appeared first on <a href="https://semiengineering.com">Semiconductor Engineering</a>.</p>
]]></description>
										<content:encoded><![CDATA[<p>With every Chimera SDK release, something quietly industrial happens: the entire 300+ model zoo gets automatically recompiled and re-profiled across a broad sweep of hardware configurations and the results land in DevStudio in the process. No one is hand-typing FPS numbers into a slide or running a single model on a single configuration to cherry-pick for a datasheet. The whole zoo moves forward together, release by release, in front of anyone who wants to look.</p>
<h2>“Port once” is not enough</h2>
<p>The traditional vendor model zoo is a curiously stale artifact: a dozen or two models get ported once, benchmarked on one silicon configuration, and the resulting FPS number gets stamped onto a product brief. Eventually the compiler evolves, a new core configuration ships, a new SoC customer wants to see the data on their target memory configuration, and the published number gradually drifts away from reality.</p>
<p>For an SoC architect, this isn&#8217;t just useless, it&#8217;s actively harmful. The number on the brief doesn&#8217;t match the chip they&#8217;re planning to license, so their engineering team has to re-run the benchmark themselves: usually under NDA, on a shrunken set of models, and only after a sales call.</p>
<p>And then for the vendor, the zoo rots between releases. Every new compiler optimization, core configuration, and model added to the zoo multiplies the manual effort required to keep the numbers fresh until eventually the math stops working and the zoo stops growing. We&#8217;ve <a href="https://quadric.ai/blog/ai-compiler-optimization-gpnpu-on-device">written before</a> about how graph compilers are still early in their maturity curve and will keep delivering performance gains release after release, but that only remains visible if the benchmark infrastructure keeps up with the compiler.</p>
<h2>Dimensioning an NPU, honestly</h2>
<p><img data-recalc-dims="1" loading="lazy" decoding="async" class="alignnone size-full wp-image-24278583" src="https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Quadric_Benchmarking-an-NPU-at-Scale-fig1.webp?resize=1434%2C807&#038;ssl=1" alt="" width="1434" height="807" srcset="https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Quadric_Benchmarking-an-NPU-at-Scale-fig1.webp?w=1434&amp;ssl=1 1434w, https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Quadric_Benchmarking-an-NPU-at-Scale-fig1.webp?resize=300%2C169&amp;ssl=1 300w, https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Quadric_Benchmarking-an-NPU-at-Scale-fig1.webp?resize=1024%2C576&amp;ssl=1 1024w, https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Quadric_Benchmarking-an-NPU-at-Scale-fig1.webp?resize=768%2C432&amp;ssl=1 768w, https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Quadric_Benchmarking-an-NPU-at-Scale-fig1.webp?resize=600%2C338&amp;ssl=1 600w" sizes="auto, (max-width: 1000px) 100vw, 1000px" /></p>
<p><strong>Fig. 1: Pass/fail status: every configuration, every product family.</strong></p>
<p><img data-recalc-dims="1" loading="lazy" decoding="async" class="alignnone size-full wp-image-24278584" src="https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Quadric_Benchmarking-an-NPU-at-Scale-fig2.webp?resize=1728%2C892&#038;ssl=1" alt="" width="1728" height="892" srcset="https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Quadric_Benchmarking-an-NPU-at-Scale-fig2.webp?w=1728&amp;ssl=1 1728w, https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Quadric_Benchmarking-an-NPU-at-Scale-fig2.webp?resize=300%2C155&amp;ssl=1 300w, https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Quadric_Benchmarking-an-NPU-at-Scale-fig2.webp?resize=1024%2C529&amp;ssl=1 1024w, https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Quadric_Benchmarking-an-NPU-at-Scale-fig2.webp?resize=768%2C396&amp;ssl=1 768w, https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Quadric_Benchmarking-an-NPU-at-Scale-fig2.webp?resize=1536%2C793&amp;ssl=1 1536w, https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Quadric_Benchmarking-an-NPU-at-Scale-fig2.webp?resize=600%2C310&amp;ssl=1 600w" sizes="auto, (max-width: 1000px) 100vw, 1000px" /></p>
<p><strong>Fig. 2: Performance across product families, core counts, MAC widths, L2 memory sizes, and bandwidths. Visit <a href="https://app.quadric.ai/nodes/quadric%2Fresnet-50/performance">DevStudio directly for live data</a>.</strong></p>
<p>With Quadric&#8217;s DevStudio, SoC sizing using the Chimera GPNPU is made concrete.</p>
<p>The greens answer the immediate question: <strong>which configurations are safe choices for my SoC?</strong> The performance matrix answers the next one: <strong>how fast do each of them go?</strong></p>
<p>The process for getting information is clean:</p>
<ul>
<li>Pick the model that matters for your product (a vision backbone, a YOLO variant, a speech or OCR model, or anything else in the zoo)</li>
<li>Pick a target Chimera product family: QC-Nano for tight power budgets, QC-Perform for mid-range, QC-Ultra for headroom</li>
<li>Select L2 memory size, MACs per PE, core count, and bandwidth</li>
</ul>
<p>From there, you can read the FPS directly and compare it to whatever peak TOPS number another vendor quoted last week, and it&#8217;s the same data our applications team uses to recommend a configuration internally. It isn&#8217;t gated behind a meeting. And that performance is a floor, not a ceiling: the SDK can profile additional configurations on demand.</p>
<p>Because every number in DevStudio comes out of the same pipeline, there&#8217;s no room for a &#8220;we got this on a good day&#8221; run or a cherry-picked configuration that flatters one model: the matrix is the matrix. It&#8217;s also how we can credibly show the compiler getting better release-over-release: the same models, the same configurations, every time.</p>
<p><img data-recalc-dims="1" loading="lazy" decoding="async" class="alignnone size-full wp-image-24278585" src="https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Quadric_Benchmarking-an-NPU-at-Scale-fig3.webp?resize=1440%2C1054&#038;ssl=1" alt="" width="1440" height="1054" srcset="https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Quadric_Benchmarking-an-NPU-at-Scale-fig3.webp?w=1440&amp;ssl=1 1440w, https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Quadric_Benchmarking-an-NPU-at-Scale-fig3.webp?resize=300%2C220&amp;ssl=1 300w, https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Quadric_Benchmarking-an-NPU-at-Scale-fig3.webp?resize=1024%2C750&amp;ssl=1 1024w, https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Quadric_Benchmarking-an-NPU-at-Scale-fig3.webp?resize=768%2C562&amp;ssl=1 768w, https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Quadric_Benchmarking-an-NPU-at-Scale-fig3.webp?resize=600%2C439&amp;ssl=1 600w" sizes="auto, (max-width: 1000px) 100vw, 1000px" /></p>
<p><strong>Fig. 3: Job log and artifact: one model, one configuration.</strong></p>
<p><img data-recalc-dims="1" loading="lazy" decoding="async" class="alignnone size-full wp-image-24278586" src="https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Quadric_Benchmarking-an-NPU-at-Scale-fig4.webp?resize=1425%2C830&#038;ssl=1" alt="" width="1425" height="830" srcset="https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Quadric_Benchmarking-an-NPU-at-Scale-fig4.webp?w=1425&amp;ssl=1 1425w, https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Quadric_Benchmarking-an-NPU-at-Scale-fig4.webp?resize=300%2C175&amp;ssl=1 300w, https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Quadric_Benchmarking-an-NPU-at-Scale-fig4.webp?resize=1024%2C596&amp;ssl=1 1024w, https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Quadric_Benchmarking-an-NPU-at-Scale-fig4.webp?resize=768%2C447&amp;ssl=1 768w, https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Quadric_Benchmarking-an-NPU-at-Scale-fig4.webp?resize=600%2C349&amp;ssl=1 600w" sizes="auto, (max-width: 1000px) 100vw, 1000px" /></p>
<p><strong>Fig. 4: Per-region profile: where compute, MAC, and memory-movement time go across the graph.</strong></p>
<p>What you&#8217;re looking at is CI/CD applied to NPU model compilation: jobs with logs and artifacts, matrix-swept across a parametric configuration space, with detailed metrics for each hardware configuration, run on every release. The pattern is the same one every serious software product uses to build and test at scale. What&#8217;s unique to Quadric is that we apply it in this domain, at this scale, out in the open for customers to see.</p>
<h2>What everybody else is doing</h2>
<p>We&#8217;ve done the homework on the rest of the NPU and edge-AI landscape, and there are three things we do that rarely show up together anywhere else: self-serve access, always-available browsing, and numbers that reflect the latest SDK.</p>
<ul>
<li><strong>Among NPU IP licensors</strong> — the vendors most directly comparable to Quadric — there is nothing self-serve. Per-model, per-configuration numbers come out of an NDA-gated engagement with the vendor&#8217;s applications team (assuming they come out at all). If they&#8217;re lucky enough to even get those numbers, the customer ends up with a one-time snapshot that can&#8217;t be re-checked against the next compiler release.</li>
<li><strong>Among edge-AI silicon vendors</strong> who do publish, the surfaces are GitHub model zoos and developer-portal model explorers, with dozens of models against a small number of fixed silicon SKUs and release cadences ranging from continuous-GitHub to roughly annual. None of them (to our knowledge) let a user self-serve a parametric IP configuration sweep on the latest release.</li>
<li><strong>Among data-center AI silicon vendors</strong>, one or two publish excellent per-model LLM matrices on a handful of fixed chips, updated continuously on GitHub. That&#8217;s a real self-serve benchmark culture, it&#8217;s just not an edge-NPU-IP configuration space.</li>
</ul>
<p>The differentiator isn&#8217;t &#8220;nobody publishes&#8221;; a few vendors do and their numbers are credible. The differentiator is combining zoo size, a parametric configuration space, and release-coupled refresh into one always-available, self-serve surface, so the numbers an SoC architect sees today are the numbers the compiler produced on the latest release and not a snapshot from a sales deck.</p>
<h2>See it for yourself</h2>
<p>The fastest way to understand what Quadric&#8217;s approach means for your SoC is to open DevStudio, pick a model you care about, and look at the configuration matrix yourself. If the one you&#8217;re planning isn&#8217;t there, you can profile it with the Chimera SDK, and if the model you&#8217;re planning isn&#8217;t in the zoo, you can upload it.</p>
<p>Check out the <a href="https://app.quadric.ai/benchmarks">Quadric DevStudio benchmarks</a> to explore a sample of the full model zoo or browse the <a href="https://app.quadric.ai/nodes">model catalog</a> to see the models Quadric validates on every SDK release.</p>
<p>Building infrastructure like this (a parametric matrix of thousands of compile+profile jobs refreshed every release, surfaced as something an SoC architect can actually use) is the sort of work we do a lot of. If that sounds like something you enjoy working on, we&#8217;d like to <a href="https://quadric.ai/careers">hear from you</a>.</p>
<p>The post <a href="https://semiengineering.com/benchmarking-an-npu-at-scale/">Benchmarking An NPU At Scale</a> appeared first on <a href="https://semiengineering.com">Semiconductor Engineering</a>.</p>
]]></content:encoded>

		<post-id xmlns="com-wordpress:feed-additions:1">24278582</post-id>	</item>
		<item>
		<title>An AI Model Fit For Purpose</title>
		<link>https://semiengineering.com/an-ai-model-fit-for-purpose/</link>
					<comments>https://semiengineering.com/an-ai-model-fit-for-purpose/#respond</comments>
		
		<dc:creator><![CDATA[Brian Bailey]]></dc:creator>
		<pubDate>Thu, 09 Jul 2026 07:01:20 +0000</pubDate>
				<category><![CDATA[Low Power-High Performance]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[Top Stories]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[AI]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[AI models]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[Axiomise]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[Cadence]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[ChipAgents]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[EDA]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[Keysight EDA]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[Mirabilis Design]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[models]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[Normal Computing]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[PPA]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[Siemens EDA]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[Synopsys]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[University of Southampton]]></category>
		<guid isPermaLink="false">https://semiengineering.com/?p=24278535</guid>

					<description><![CDATA[<p>Using models outside the context in which they were created for can result in misleading information. </p>
<p>The post <a href="https://semiengineering.com/an-ai-model-fit-for-purpose/">An AI Model Fit For Purpose</a> appeared first on <a href="https://semiengineering.com">Semiconductor Engineering</a>.</p>
]]></description>
										<content:encoded><![CDATA[<p><strong>Key takeaways</strong></p>
<ul>
<li><span data-contrast="auto">A model can only be used for its intended purpose, in a defined context, without taking unknown risks.</span><span data-ccp-props="{&quot;335559739&quot;:0}"> </span></li>
<li><span data-contrast="auto">Models must be created using a well-defined process and verified in a way that provides a level of independence.</span><span data-ccp-props="{&quot;335559739&quot;:0}"> </span></li>
<li><span data-contrast="auto">Deployment requires trust and a way to track the properties of the model.</span></li>
</ul>
<hr />
<p><span data-contrast="auto">A model captures some kind of behavior exhibited in the real world, but all models are compromises. They sacrifice some detail for execution performance. </span><span data-ccp-props="{&quot;335559739&quot;:0}"> </span></p>
<p><span data-contrast="auto">The most important model arguably is the design, followed by a collection of models that form the verification process. But it also could be a model of a tool or a flow. AI is transforming the creation and utilization of these models.</span><span data-ccp-props="{&quot;335559739&quot;:0}"> </span></p>
<p><span data-contrast="auto">Surrogate models of the design are often created to increase execution speed, leaving out some detail, or being approximate in nature. In many cases, the models are constructed hierarchically. An RTL model, for example, is a clock-accurate model of the design that utilizes gate or arithmetic-level models between clocked edges, which are themselves idealized from transistor models that are simplified models of physics. Each abstraction works so long as it is not used out of the context for which it was designed. Such models are used in both the analog and digital spaces. [An in-depth discussion of these can be found in &#8216;</span><a href="https://semiengineering.com/can-ai-create-missing-models/"><span data-contrast="none">Can AI Create Missing Models?</span></a><span data-contrast="auto">&#8216;]</span><span data-ccp-props="{&quot;335559739&quot;:0}"> </span></p>
<p><span data-contrast="auto">Model creation is generally expensive, in part because it needs to be verified. Models with unknown accuracy or fidelity, or used in a context outside of the original intent, can lead to bad decisions or missed problems. Both of these issues can be very expensive to correct if found later in the flow, or worse, if they escape into a product. If AI starts creating models, trust must precede adoption, and that only happens when a repeatable and traceable process is used. Even then, tagging is essential for attaching metadata to models to define their scope and suitability.</span><span data-ccp-props="{&quot;335559739&quot;:0}"> </span></p>
<p><span data-contrast="auto">Examples of tool and flow modeling include DSO.ai from </span>Synopsys<span data-contrast="auto">, and Cerebrus from </span>Cadence<span data-contrast="auto">. These create optimization loops for block-based physical implementation tools based on reinforcement learning. After each trial, the PPA can be measured, and that new datapoint is used to improve the AI model. This influences a more informed choice of tool settings for the given design.</span><span data-ccp-props="{&quot;335559739&quot;:0}"> </span></p>
<p><span data-contrast="auto">The primary input for these implementation optimization tools is the RTL design model. At that stage in the flow, it is fairly stable. Many of the AI models being created today are tackling areas of the design flow where things are not so stable, such as verification. Here, the point is to find errors in either the design or the testbench, and to correct them as quickly as possible while converging on a coverage goal for a target that may also be moving. Understanding the correctness and lifetime of the data being used to create the models becomes a very important part of the process.</span><span data-ccp-props="{&quot;335559739&quot;:0}"> </span></p>
<p><span data-contrast="auto">Given that verification is a convergence process, it does provide some level of forgiveness in terms of data validity. &#8220;You can only really measure accuracy on in-domain data or ground truth simulations, which are expensive, so generalization is a challenge,&#8221; says Arvind Srinivasan, product engineering lead for </span>Normal Computing<span data-contrast="auto">. &#8220;If you train a very small model as a proxy for something else through imitation learning, as soon as the original representation changes structurally, you are somewhere else. There are two alternatives. First, learn physical rules that generalize, in which case the complexity is too high, and the only way you can do that is to over-reduce to something under-parameterized and over-broad. Second, remove the explicit physical constraints and try to learn via semantic understanding and use world models or LLMs to transfer knowledge.&#8221;</span><span data-ccp-props="{&quot;335559739&quot;:0}"> </span></p>
<p><span data-contrast="auto">Tagging of analog behavioral models is equally important so that the model is not used in places where aspects of a model are required but not verified.</span><span data-ccp-props="{&quot;335559739&quot;:0}"> </span></p>
<p aria-level="2"><strong>Creating models for design and verification</strong><span data-ccp-props="{&quot;134245418&quot;:true,&quot;134245529&quot;:true,&quot;335559738&quot;:160,&quot;335559739&quot;:80}"><strong> </strong><br />
</span><span data-contrast="auto">Many processes have been developed for model creation, and they will certainly change in the AI era. For purposes of this discussion, a fairly classical one is used. Mehir Arora, head of engineering at </span><a href="https://semiengineering.com/entities/alpha-design-ai-chipagents/">ChipAgents</a><span data-contrast="auto"> proposes a six-step process:</span><span data-ccp-props="{&quot;335559739&quot;:0}"> </span></p>
<ul>
<li><span data-contrast="auto">Define the model&#8217;s scope, interface, accuracy requirements and use context; </span><span data-ccp-props="{&quot;335559739&quot;:0}"> </span></li>
<li><span data-contrast="auto">Generate reference data via golden simulations;</span><span data-ccp-props="{&quot;335559739&quot;:0}"> </span></li>
<li><span data-contrast="auto">Produce the behavioral model;</span><span data-ccp-props="{&quot;335559739&quot;:0}"> </span></li>
<li><span data-contrast="auto">Validate against the golden model;</span><span data-ccp-props="{&quot;335559739&quot;:0}"> </span></li>
<li><span data-contrast="auto">Refine through iteration, and</span><span data-ccp-props="{&quot;335559739&quot;:0}"> </span></li>
<li><span data-contrast="auto">Deploy into the simulation environment.</span><span data-ccp-props="{&quot;335559739&quot;:0}"> </span></li>
</ul>
<p><span data-contrast="auto">Knowing how and when the model is going to be used is important, not only for model accuracy, but also ensuring that you minimize the work required to make the model. &#8220;You could have a very bespoke model that only your tool can use, or you can produce a model in the form of a SPICE network that gets wider deployment,&#8221; says Tom Demuer, research and development fellow at </span><a href="https://semiengineering.com/entities/keysight-technologies/">Keysight EDA</a><span data-contrast="auto">. &#8220;There are very different effort levels between delivering a complete Verilog model, or just picking the simplest one that can perform the job.&#8221;</span><span data-ccp-props="{&quot;335559739&quot;:0}"> </span></p>
<p><span data-contrast="auto">There are two important axes that have to be considered — accuracy and completeness. &#8220;They are different, and both matter,&#8221; says Ashish Darbari, CEO at </span><a href="https://semiengineering.com/entities/axiomise/">Axiomise</a><span data-contrast="auto">. &#8220;In digital verification, accuracy means comparing the model&#8217;s recommendations or predictions with outcomes we already trust. Completeness is harder because it is tied to what part of the verification problem the model is helping us cover. A model may look good on common scenarios, yet still miss rare protocol interactions, reset behavior, low-power modes, or security-sensitive paths.&#8221;</span><span data-ccp-props="{&quot;335559739&quot;:0}"> </span></p>
<p><span data-contrast="auto">In the analog world, things are slightly different. &#8220;Accuracy is a statistical question,&#8221; says Hanna Yip, product manager at Normal Computing. &#8220;How close are the model&#8217;s outputs to ground truth across some distribution? Completeness is a coverage question. Does the model have behavior defined for all the scenarios that matter? You can have a highly accurate model that is deeply incomplete, one that produces outputs close to ground truth for everything it covers, but which has no behavior for important scenarios. Those two failure modes require different remedies.&#8221;</span><span data-ccp-props="{&quot;335559739&quot;:0}"> </span></p>
<p><span data-contrast="auto">Knowing the usage context can help define the attributes of a model. &#8220;Correct use begins with defining the role of the model,&#8221; says Axiomise&#8217;s Darbari. &#8220;In digital verification, many of these are advisory flow models, not signoff engines. They can recommend regressions, prioritize tests, suggest assertions, or guide proof strategy, but they should not be allowed to declare closure on their own. That means putting them inside a bounded decision loop where simulation, emulation, formal results, or other trusted analyses remain the final authority.&#8221;</span><span data-ccp-props="{&quot;335559739&quot;:0}"> </span></p>
<p><span data-contrast="auto">The creation of a model from data is similar to the problem of creating a model from a specification. &#8220;I want to give a spec to my large language model, and I want to have it generate a model,&#8221; says Paul Graykowski, director of product marketing at </span><a href="https://semiengineering.com/entities/cadence-design-systems/">Cadence</a><span data-contrast="auto">. &#8220;How do you guide the LLM in the right direction to generate code that&#8217;s valid, especially given that most specs are not very good? It doesn&#8217;t matter what you feed it — specs, timing diagrams — they all form part of a mental model, and there are a lot of things that must be explored that a spec maybe takes for granted.&#8221;</span><span data-ccp-props="{&quot;335559739&quot;:0}"> </span></p>
<p><b><span data-contrast="auto">Creating a golden model</span></b><span data-ccp-props="{&quot;335559739&quot;:0}"> </span><span data-contrast="auto"><br />
Assuming that the required model is going to be created from samples, simulations of a trusted model are used. &#8220;The quality of the training distribution matters as much as the volume of training data,&#8221; says Normal&#8217;s Yip. &#8220;For DRAM verification, the failure modes you care about, such as timing violations, protocol corner cases, or conditional interactions, are systematically underrepresented in typical simulation traces because most simulation time is spent on nominal scenarios. A model trained on that data will be confidently accurate on common cases, and silently wrong on the cases that cause re-spins. A deliberate sampling strategy in the data is as important as the fitting method, and it requires domain knowledge to get right.&#8221;</span><span data-ccp-props="{&quot;335559739&quot;:0}"> </span></p>
<p><span data-contrast="auto">Analog creation is more physics-facing. &#8220;There, engineers run SPICE or EM across selected corners and stimuli, then train a surrogate or reduced-order model and package it as Verilog-A, real-number modeling, or another behavioral form,&#8221; says Darbari. &#8220;In both cases, the principle is the same: learn from trusted reference data and integrate the result back into the model.&#8221;</span><span data-ccp-props="{&quot;335559739&quot;:0}"> </span></p>
<p><span data-contrast="auto">This flow may be suitable for a single model, but things become more complicated when a collection of models is involved, as would exist within a verification flow. Multiple data sources, multiple tools and multiple models are being continuously improved. &#8220;These models are usually created from data the verification flow already produces, including regression histories, coverage databases, debug outcomes, waveform traces, property results, and tool logs,&#8221; says Darbari. &#8220;That data is cleaned, labeled, and used to train ranking, classification, or recommendation models that can predict which tests matter, which failures are related, or where proof convergence is most likely. In formal, the inputs often include assertions, protocol specifications, abstract models, and prior proof results, so the AI is learning not just from simulation history but also from verification intent.&#8221;</span><span data-ccp-props="{&quot;335559739&quot;:0}"> </span></p>
<p><span data-contrast="auto">From the reference data, a model can be created. &#8220;The agents analyze it to extract key parameters for the behavioral model,&#8221; says ChipAgents&#8217; Arora. &#8220;Then, we iteratively produce a Verilog-A model that captures a subset of the behavior reflected in the reference data.&#8221;</span><span data-ccp-props="{&quot;335559739&quot;:0}"> </span></p>
<p><span data-contrast="auto">None of the generation tools available in the market today is perfect. &#8220;Accuracy measurement for AI-generated design content requires distinguishing three properties that are not equivalent — syntactic correctness, functional correctness, and formal completeness,&#8221; says Simon Davidmann, AI and EDA researcher at the University of Southampton. &#8220;Treating these as interchangeable is one of the most consequential evaluation errors a team can make.&#8221;</span><span data-ccp-props="{&quot;335559739&quot;:0}"> </span></p>
<p><span data-contrast="auto">We can expect varying degrees of correctness on this. &#8220;I can have an LLM write C or Python, and it&#8217;s going to be pretty accurate and run out of the box,&#8221; says Cadence&#8217;s Graykowski. &#8220;But if I am talking about SystemVerilog assertions or really complex UVM, it&#8217;s going to be hit or miss. And even though they&#8217;re all written to the SystemVerilog standard, the standard is kind of loose. It&#8217;s not perfect. The tools will give their best attempt at it.&#8221;</span><span data-ccp-props="{&quot;335559739&quot;:0}"> </span></p>
<p><span data-contrast="auto">Others point to similar gaps. &#8220;Syntactic correctness, meaning whether the generated code compiles without errors, is a necessary but insufficient measure,&#8221; said Davidmann. &#8220;Current LLMs achieve near-perfect syntactic correctness on standard Verilog constructs. Functional correctness, measured by pass@k on benchmark suites such as VerilogEval, represents the fraction of generated modules that pass functional simulation within k attempts. However, these benchmarks are dominated by short, pedagogical-style modules that do not represent industrial SoC subsystem complexity. A model scoring 90% on VerilogEval may never have been trained on a cache controller with non-trivial CDC handling. The benchmarks in use today were designed for a world where humans wrote RTL. The field needs benchmarks designed to find out where AI breaks, and those benchmarks do not yet exist at industrial scale.&#8221;</span><span data-ccp-props="{&quot;335559739&quot;:0}"> </span></p>
<p><span data-contrast="auto">There are also important techniques that can increase the chances of creating a good model. &#8220;If you can reduce to very compact representations, you are more likely to generalize because you probably learned something more fundamental,&#8221; says Thomas Ahle, head of machine learning at Normal. &#8220;Google recently released a strong weather model where they used very small bottleneck layers forcing the model to find a compact way of representing things, trying to discover the real physics of the system rather than learning a hundred million rules of thumb.&#8221;</span><span data-ccp-props="{&quot;335559739&quot;:0}"> </span></p>
<p><b><span data-contrast="auto">Validating against a golden model</span></b><span data-ccp-props="{&quot;335559739&quot;:0}"> </span><span data-contrast="auto"><br />
A common mistake is to use the same examples for verification that were used for training. &#8220;Accuracy can be verified by creating and running sanity test cases where we compare the generated stats against expected values and check if they match,&#8221; says Deepak Shankar, founder of </span>Mirabilis Design<span data-contrast="auto">. &#8220;Generated models need to be manually validated to make sure the AI models don&#8217;t hallucinate as the model becomes big.&#8221;</span><span data-ccp-props="{&quot;335559739&quot;:0}"> </span></p>
<p><span data-contrast="auto">Sanity tests are important. &#8220;If you have S parameter data that you&#8217;re trying to model for some linear system — let&#8217;s say a filter — you will typically do a training set, but keep a holdout set so that you can validate whether your model matches the holdout set,&#8221; says Keysight&#8217;s Demuer. &#8220;Another important test is making sure that the surrogate model behaves physically. For instance, if you have a passive structure like a filter, the first thing to validate would be that your model keeps being passive. That&#8217;s a good litmus test. Similarly, your model needs to remain causal. You cannot traverse backwards in time.&#8221;</span><span data-ccp-props="{&quot;335559739&quot;:0}"> </span></p>
<p><span data-contrast="auto">It is also important to recognize that, by definition, the models are not accurate outside of their intended purpose. &#8220;In behavioral models, waveforms do not necessarily need to match perfectly,&#8221; says Arora. &#8220;The model is meant to be a simplification of the true circuit behavior. Thus, the agent spends much of this phase reasoning about whether the deviations from the golden simulation are justified by the constraints of the model.&#8221;</span><span data-ccp-props="{&quot;335559739&quot;:0}"> </span></p>
<p><span data-contrast="auto">You have to judge fitness for purpose. &#8220;On the analog side, behavioral and surrogate models simplify circuit behavior by design, so they can miss subtle effects or behavior outside the training envelope,&#8221; says Darbari. &#8220;These models may be powerful accelerators, but they do not remove the need for strong specifications, engineering judgment, and independent signoff evidence.&#8221;</span><span data-ccp-props="{&quot;335559739&quot;:0}"> </span></p>
<p><span data-contrast="auto">There are various ways to help identify holes in coverage. &#8220;Along with the model, we generate plots that demonstrate the generated model’s behavior in comparison to the ground truth,&#8221; says Sidharth Kannan, </span><span data-contrast="none">agent research engineer </span><span data-contrast="auto">at </span>ChipAgents<span data-contrast="auto">. &#8220;Human engineers are then required to sign off on the validity of the model before the agent is permitted to terminate. Every model and its outputs should be traceable and reproducible.&#8221;</span><span data-ccp-props="{&quot;335559739&quot;:0}"> </span></p>
<p><span data-contrast="auto">When inaccuracies, errors, or problems are found, iteration is required. &#8220;This is where agents can dramatically reduce the burden,&#8221; says ChipAgents&#8217; Arora. &#8220;They can perform automated gap detection where agents compare model against golden, identify where errors are highest, and automatically produce new tests to understand behavior in those regions. They can conduct active learning. Instead of running a predefined grid of simulations, agents can intelligently select the next simulation point that maximizes information gain. Then, once new simulation data is collected, agents can recreate the model and re-evaluate — without engineer intervention.&#8221;</span><span data-ccp-props="{&quot;335559739&quot;:0}"> </span></p>
<p><span data-contrast="auto">Alternative flows attempt to incrementally build the model over time. This is especially true when modeling tools and flows, because it is not possible to build up a complete model ahead of time. That means refinement and deployment become tightly linked. &#8220;They learn from historical data, so they are often better at finding more of what the team has already seen than at exposing genuinely novel bugs or corner cases,&#8221; says Darbari. &#8220;They can improve coverage growth, but coverage is not the same as correctness, and strong-looking metrics can still hide important escapes.&#8221;</span><span data-ccp-props="{&quot;335559739&quot;:0}"> </span></p>
<p><b><span data-contrast="auto">Simulation is essential</span></b><span data-ccp-props="{&quot;335559739&quot;:0}"> </span><span data-contrast="auto"><br />
Before deployment, there needs to be some kind of sign-off. &#8220;Before adopting any AI model into a production flow, the right question is not only how accurate the model is, but what it was trained on, what that distribution does not cover, and how close the actual design is to that boundary,&#8221; says Southampton&#8217;s Davidmann. &#8220;Every team deploying these tools should run a deliberate boundary-probing exercise on a representative sample of their most complex and novel design patterns, verifying the outputs independently against what a domain expert would produce. That exercise identifies where the distribution ends and the model&#8217;s uncertainty begins. Without it, deployment is proceeding without the information needed to manage the risk.&#8221;</span><span data-ccp-props="{&quot;335559739&quot;:0}"> </span></p>
<p><span data-contrast="auto">It is important to understand the properties of the generated model. &#8220;Usually, it starts by tagging the model with properties,&#8221; says Keysight&#8217;s Demuer. &#8220;For instance, it&#8217;s a guaranteed passive model, it&#8217;s a guaranteed causal model. The next one might be where it is applicable — the parameter range under which you can trust results to be fair. After that, there is not much maturity in how to label a model in terms of accuracy, or the balance between accuracy and speed. What I&#8217;ve seen is that most teams have an underlying assumption that the model can be used and has been developed by a team that gives them good results.&#8221;</span><span data-ccp-props="{&quot;335559739&quot;:0}"> </span></p>
<p><span data-contrast="auto">It can get complicated when there are restrictions on the data used to create the model. &#8220;It is important to put a watermark on any AI-generated content,&#8221; says Sathishkumar Balasubramanian, head of products at </span><a href="https://semiengineering.com/entities/mentor-a-siemens-business/">Siemens EDA</a><span data-contrast="auto">. &#8220;Google just came up with a standard for any content that is AI-generated so that people know that it&#8217;s coming from an LLM. Having good data labeling is going to be very important. When you&#8217;re feeding data into your fine-tuned model, you have to make sure that you&#8217;re able to authenticate proper data. You can&#8217;t just say, &#8216;Go do whatever you want. Grab any data you want.&#8217; You have to have a process to validate the data before you start fine-tuning it. It requires discipline, and you&#8217;ve got to do it all the way from the source.&#8221;</span><span data-ccp-props="{&quot;134233279&quot;:false,&quot;335559739&quot;:160}"> </span></p>
<p><span data-contrast="auto">That implies trust. &#8220;I would not trust them 100%,&#8221; adds Demure. &#8220;Even in workflows where they are critical, they will typically be used in a hybrid form, where you&#8217;re using the AI-generated models. But you do, for instance, 50 evaluations of the AI model, and one with the true ground truth, so that you can validate over time whether you&#8217;re still within what you think you should have.&#8221;</span><span data-ccp-props="{&quot;335559739&quot;:0}"> </span></p>
<p><span data-contrast="auto">Without trust, adoption will be limited. &#8220;One of the key properties of AI models in EDA is that reliability is bounded by training data distribution,&#8221; says Davidmann. &#8220;This constraint is well understood in machine learning theory, but rarely documented in vendor tool datasheets, and it has direct consequences for how these tools should be evaluated and deployed. Most teams do not yet have a standard for documenting which assertions are human-authored and which are AI-generated, or what that distinction means for sign-off confidence. In addition, most vendor documentation does not specify the training scope, failure modes, or domain boundaries of the models being deployed. Both gaps need to close before AI-generated content scales into safety-critical designs.&#8221;</span><span data-ccp-props="{&quot;335559739&quot;:0}"> </span></p>
<p>The post <a href="https://semiengineering.com/an-ai-model-fit-for-purpose/">An AI Model Fit For Purpose</a> appeared first on <a href="https://semiengineering.com">Semiconductor Engineering</a>.</p>
]]></content:encoded>

					<link>https://www.eetimes.com/alok-jain-the-engineer-who-never-wanted-to-be-a-manager/</link>

						<![CDATA[<p>Meet Alok Jain, the reluctant manager who turned Cadence India into a chip-design powerhouse—and see why AI is his next bet.</p>
<p>The post <a href="https://www.eetimes.com/alok-jain-the-engineer-who-never-wanted-to-be-a-manager/">Alok Jain: The Engineer Who Never Wanted to Be a Manager</a> appeared first on <a href="https://www.eetimes.com">EE Times</a>.</p>

								<![CDATA[<p>Few people spend three decades helping shape the engineering backbone of a global technology company. Fewer still witness an entire country&#8217;s transformation into a critical node of the global semiconductor</p>]]>
